analysis.device: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition

bottleneck.description: The entire compute budget runs in FP32, which forces both the projection GEMMs and the attention kernel off the Tensor Core pipeline. All 7 projection matmuls are served by the CUTLASS kernel cutlass_80_simt_sgemm_128x256_8x4_tn_align1 -- the 'simt' substring confirms it executes on the SIMT/CUDA-core datapath, not the HMMA Tensor Core datapath. tensor_core_active_pct is exactly 0.0 and smsp__pipe_tensor_cycles_active.avg is 0 on every mm kernel, so the Tensor Cores are completely idle while the SIMT path runs at only ~36% SM throughput and 16.6% achieved occupancy. The two attention ops use fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80 -- the f32 mem-efficient kernel -- which is the slowest attention backend and also bypasses Tensor Cores. FP32 is the single root cause spanning ~89% of attributed time (419k ns of GEMM + 221k ns of attention).

transformation.description: Insert dtype conversion so the GEMM and attention inputs are bf16 (preferred on Blackwell) instead of fp32. In the Aten IR this means inserting aten::_to_copy / aten::convert_element_type on the activation feeding each aten::mm.default and on the q/k/v operands of aten::_scaled_dot_product_efficient_attention.default, and casting weight placeholders to bf16. bf16 inputs route the CUTLASS GEMM selection to a Tensor Core HMMA kernel (a tensor-op tile instead of simt_sgemm), engaging the otherwise-idle Tensor Cores, and route attention to the faster fp16/bf16 mem-efficient (or flash) fmha kernel. This also halves DRAM and L2 traffic for weights and activations.
transformation.location: Aten IR graph, activation and weight edges feeding all aten::mm.default and aten::_scaled_dot_product_efficient_attention.default nodes
fx_steps: for node in graph.nodes: identify node.target == torch.ops.aten.mm.default and node.target == torch.ops.aten._scaled_dot_product_efficient_attention.default

for each fp32 weight placeholder feeding an mm: with graph.inserting_after(weight): cast = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten._to_copy.default, args=(weight,), kwargs={'dtype': torch.bfloat16}); weight.replace_all_uses_with(cast) then re-point cast's own arg back to weight
for each activation input to mm/SDPA: insert torch.ops.aten._to_copy.default to torch.bfloat16 before the consuming node via graph.inserting_before(node)
insert a single torch.ops.aten._to_copy.default back to fp32 on the final output node so the external contract is preserved
graph.lint(); recompile via torch._inductor.compile_fx.compile_fx so Inductor re-selects Tensor Core templates
transformation.code_hint: Prefer wrapping the module with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16) at trace time, or apply an Inductor pre-grad pass that promotes mm/SDPA operands to bf16; verify the recompiled GEMM kernel name no longer contains 'simt_sgemm' and that tensor_core_active_pct > 0.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: ~50% on weight/activation reads (fp32->bf16); DRAM read per GEMM ~9.5 -> ~4.8 units

optimizations.notes: Mechanism (Tensor Core engagement) is certain from tensor_core_active_pct=0 plus the simt_sgemm kernel name. Magnitude is an estimate: SIMT-SGEMM->HMMA typically yields 3-6x on the GEMMs and ~2-3x on attention; duration_ns is ncu-inflated 2-5x so treat the 64% figure as relative. Must precede any pass that calls register_buffer (OPT-2, OPT-3), because buffers allocated post-promotion cannot be recast.

operators: aten::mm (op_id=4 Q, op_id=5 K, op_id=6 V; op_id=26/27/28 block 2) (6 nodes)

bottleneck.description: Q, K and V are three independent, bias-free Linear projections that each lower to a separate aten::mm.default with identical shapes [4096,512] x [512,512]. They share the same activation input (the layer-norm output; input_op_ids (0,-1) for op_id 4/5/6). Running them as three serial kernels triples kernel-launch overhead and yields three medium-sized GEMMs each at only 16.6% achieved occupancy and ~36% SM throughput -- the tiles do not fill the 188-SM Blackwell device well. With grid_dim [64,1,2] each launch produces only 128 CTAs, far below one wave on this GPU, so the device is under-occupied per launch.

transformation.description: Concatenate the three [512,512] projection weights into a single [512,1536] (out_features 3*512) weight and replace the three aten::mm.default nodes that share the same activation input with one aten::mm.default of [4096,512] x [512,1536], then split the [4096,1536] result back into Q/K/V via three aten::slice.default (or a single view+unbind) feeding the existing reshape/SDPA path. One larger GEMM presents a bigger tile to CUTLASS, raising occupancy and SM throughput, and replaces 3 launches with 1 per block (6 -> 2 across the two blocks).
transformation.location: Aten IR graph, the three sibling aten::mm.default nodes per block whose input_op_ids point to the same native_layer_norm output
fx_steps: register_buffer('qkv_weight', torch.cat([q_w, k_w, v_w], dim=0)) in bf16 (after OPT-1 promotion)
locate the 3 aten::mm.default nodes sharing activation X; with graph.inserting_before(first_mm): fused = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.mm.default, args=(X, qkv_weight_t))
qkv = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.split.Tensor, args=(fused, 512), kwargs={'dim': 1})  # or aten::slice.default x3
erase the 3 original mm nodes and their dead weight placeholders; graph.lint()
transformation.code_hint: Match by: same activation source node + identical [512,512] weight shape + no bias add consumer. Apply per transformer block; keep the concatenated weight contiguous so the resulting GEMM stays on the Tensor Core HMMA path selected in OPT-1.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Activation X read once instead of 3x per block; weight reads coalesced into one contiguous fetch

optimizations.notes: Depends on OPT-1: the concatenated qkv buffer is register_buffer'd and must be created at the bf16 runtime dtype. The primary speedup is the GEMM dtype change (OPT-1); fusion adds launch-overhead reduction and better tiling on top. Standard QKV-fusion pattern, hence high confidence.

bottleneck.description: The selected CUTLASS GEMM kernel is cutlass_80_simt_sgemm_128x256_8x4_tn_align1. The 'tn' layout indicates the weight is consumed transposed and 'align1' signals a 1-element (4-byte) global-load alignment guarantee, which prevents the vectorized 128-bit loads CUTLASS prefers. Combined with l1tex t_sector_hit_rate of only ~10.8% on the GEMM kernels, the load path is under-vectorized. Pre-transposing/forcing-contiguous the weight so the GEMM sees an aligned, already-transposed operand lets CUTLASS pick a higher-alignment (align8) tile.

transformation.description: Pre-transpose the projection weights at compile time and store them contiguous with 16-byte alignment so the lowered aten::mm.default consumes an aligned, pre-transposed operand. After OPT-1 (bf16) this allows CUTLASS to select an align8 Tensor Core tile instead of align1, enabling 128-bit vector loads and reducing register pressure (currently 210 regs/thread caps occupancy at 16.6%).
transformation.location: Aten IR weight operands of aten::mm.default (post QKV fusion: the single qkv_weight and the output-projection weight)
fx_steps: for each weight feeding aten::mm.default: w_t = torch.ops.aten.t.default(w) folded at trace time, stored via register_buffer as a contiguous bf16 tensor (after OPT-1 and OPT-2)
rewrite the mm node args to consume the pre-transposed contiguous buffer so the 'tn' transpose is eliminated and alignment is raised
constant-fold the transpose with torch._inductor so it is not re-executed each iteration; graph.lint()
transformation.code_hint: Verify the recompiled kernel name alignment suffix increases (align1 -> align8) and that l1tex sector hit rate / SM throughput rise. Low risk but contingent on Inductor's template selection actually honoring the alignment hint.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Higher load vectorization (align1->align8); fewer redundant transpose materializations

optimizations.notes: Depends on OPT-1 (dtype) and OPT-2 (QKV fusion eliminates the original Q/K/V weight placeholders, so pre-transpose must act on the fused weight). Confidence is low: with bf16 in place CUTLASS may already pick an aligned tile, making this redundant; treat as a follow-up only if profiling after OPT-1/OPT-2 still shows an align1 kernel.

global_notes: knowledge/hardware-limits.md and knowledge/fx-patterns.md were not present in the repository; per documented fallback, FX API patterns were drawn from established torch.fx / Inductor knowledge and any novel transform was capped at medium confidence. Hardware context for the RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition (GB202, ~188 SMs) is assumed from public spec.
0 operators below the 1% threshold were omitted (all 12 distinct granular operators are >=1%).

fused_kernel_double_count: the operator 'layer::unique::prologue' (is_fused=true, 21 kernels, 1,085,056 ns) is an NVTX layer-wrapper that re-attributes the same Triton/CUTLASS/fmha kernels already counted in the 12 granular operators. It was EXCLUDED from the time budget to avoid double counting; total_attributed_ns = 719,712 ns over the 14 granular kernels.

warm_up_inflation (tentative): the prologue range captured ~21 kernels (multiple forward passes' worth) while the granular mm operators each show kernel_count=1 and layer_norm/unsafe_view show kernel_count=2. The uneven counts suggest the measured-iteration boundary and warm-up are partially conflated; relative per-op comparisons remain valid but absolute kernel_counts should not be multiplied by measure_iters.
duration_ns values are inflated 2-5x by ncu counter collection; all reduction figures are relative within this profile, not absolute latencies.

Both _efficient_attention_forward operators have empty raw metrics (ncu collected no counters for the fmha kernel); the FP32 conclusion for attention rests on the kernel name (fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned) and its 110k-ns duration, not on counters.

Dependency DAG (no cycles): OPT-1 (dtype promotion) -> OPT-2 (QKV fusion) -> OPT-3 (pre-transpose). OPT-1 must run first because OPT-2 and OPT-3 register_buffer concatenated/transposed weights that must be allocated at the bf16 runtime dtype. The model already emits aten::_scaled_dot_product_efficient_attention, so no separate SDPA-formation pass is needed; the attention win comes entirely from OPT-1's dtype change routing it to a faster fmha backend.
